EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin interacted with a stationary front to cause heavy rain producing thunderstorms over portions of northern Ohio.  The thunderstorms trained across Wyanodot, Hancock, Crawford and Richland Counties during the early morning hours of August 21st.  Catastrophic flooding occurred in all of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell across the area over the past few days eventually ran off into the Killbuck Creek. During the morning hours of the 21st, the Killbuck Creek at Killbuck went into flood. Flood stage there is 15.0 feet. The river continued rising during the afternoon of the 21st, and crested during the overnight hours into the 22nd at around 16.2 feet. Because of the heavy runoff throughout the basin, the river remained in flood through the day on the 22nd, and then finally fell back below flood stage during the early morning hours of the 23rd. While much of the floodwaters from the Killbuck River mainly flow into farm fields nearby, some streets and homes within Killbuck were still affected by the river going above flood stage. The flash flood write up for Holmes County contains much of the estimated damage from flash flooding on the 21st. Damage estimates for this event describe damage directly associated to the river going into flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin interacted with a stationary front to cause heavy rain producing thunderstorms over portions of northern Ohio.  The thunderstorms trained across Wyanodot, Hancock, Crawford and Richland Counties during the early morning hours of August 21st.  Catastrophic flooding occurred in all of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding conditions persisted throughout the county long after flash flooding occurred during the morning hours of the 21st. By the afternoon of the 21st, numerous roads were closed and much of the evacuations took place to get people away from dangerous floodwaters. Road closures along with flooded homes and businesses continued into the 22nd, but by the evening hours high water began receding across the county. Reports from the early morning hours of the 23rd indicate that all roads were reopened by that time. The only remaining closed roads and bridges were ones that sustained severe structural damage. See the accompanying flash flood write up for Ashland County for more details including damage estimates from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin interacted with a stationary front to cause heavy rain producing thunderstorms over portions of northern Ohio.  The thunderstorms trained across Wyanodot, Hancock, Crawford and Richland Counties during the early morning hours of August 21st.  Catastrophic flooding occurred in all of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding throughout Richland County continued for several days after flash flooding occurred during the morning hours of the 21st. By the afternoon of the 21st, shelters for displaced flood victims opened across the county. Numerous roads were closed through the overnight hours as floodwaters remained high. Throughout the day on the 22nd, roads remained closed, and home and business were still flooded. Conditions started improving during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd as water began receding. This trend continued during the overnight hours, and conditions improved enough by the morning of the 23rd for roads to open and people to return to their homes and businesses. The only remaining closed roads and bridges were ones that sustained severe structural damage. See the accompanying flash flood write up for more details about this event, along with damage estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin interacted with a stationary front to cause heavy rain producing thunderstorms over portions of northern Ohio.  The thunderstorms trained across Wyanodot, Hancock, Crawford and Richland Counties during the early morning hours of August 21st.  Catastrophic flooding occurred in all of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the early morning hours of the 21st. During the early morning hours reports described roads with high water on them. As the morning progressed, water quickly flowed into streams and rivers across the county, and those quickly rose. By mid-morning, a campground in Batemantown near the north branch of the Kokosing River was evacuated because the river rose approximately eight inches in just two hours. As the day progressed, there were no additional reports of evacuations, and no roads were closed by high water. By the afternoon, only a few minor roads still had water on them.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was oriented west to east across central Ohio. The surface frontal boundary, in combination with upper level atmospheric support, allowed for severe thunderstorms to develop across the region. Many of the thunderstorms that day were severe, and even a few tornadoes developed across northern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved into Marion around 4:10 pm. Numerous trees, large limbs, utility poles and power lines came down during the storm. Reports indicate that many trees fell onto homes, garages and cars throughout the city. One of the houses was damaged enough to be considered destroyed. Between 30 and 35 utility poles had to be replaced because of trees and large limbs falling onto them. One house had three trees fall onto it causing extensive damage. A house in Marion that was being remodeled sustained damage by trees. Three people working on the house quickly took shelter on a porch during the storm, but were injuried when a tree fell onto the roof of the porch and collapsed the roof. A twenty-two year old girl was killed when a large tree limb fell onto the car while she was driving. A passenger in the vehicle survived with injuries. ||Additional support was called in to help clean up in Marion. Electric companies and municipal agencies coordinated to help clean up debris. Estimated costs for clean up in Marion were approximately $91 thousand.  369 loads of debris were cleaned up throughout the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin interacted with a stationary front to cause heavy rain producing thunderstorms over portions of northern Ohio.  The thunderstorms trained across Wyanodot, Hancock, Crawford and Richland Counties during the early morning hours of August 21st.  Catastrophic flooding occurred in all of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms affected Crawford County during the late evening hours of August 20th and early morning hours of August 21st.  Rainfall rates with the strongest storms exceeded three inches per hour.  Trained spotters in Bucyrus measured storm totals of 8.68 inches and 8.5 inches respectively.  About 5 inches of this total fell between midnight and 4 am.  Runoff from this rain combined with ground already saturated from earlier rains led to catastrophic flooding across portions of Crawford County.  Local officials stated that the flooding in the county was the worst ever!  A total of 68 homes were destroyed with another 240 homes suffering major structural damage.  Another 600 homes sustained minor damage with over 1200 additional homes affected (less than three feet of water in the basement).  Many businesses also sustained significant damage.  Some of the worst flooding occurred in Bucyrus were many people had to be rescued from flood cars.  Hundreds of people had to be evacuated and including at least 30 that had to be rescued by boat.  Crestline was also hard hit with water in some part of the town over waist deep.  Significant damage was reported along Bucyrus Street and Thrush Ave.  At least one business in Crestline was destroyed.  Dozens of roads and streets had to be closed because of flooding.  Damage to roads, bridges and culverts was extensive.  Cleanup and overtime costs incurred by local government agencies was substantial.  Finally, standing water and erosion from the runoff caused damage to agricultural interests in the county.   |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin interacted with a stationary front to cause heavy rain producing thunderstorms over portions of northern Ohio.  The thunderstorms trained across Wyanodot, Hancock, Crawford and Richland Counties during the early morning hours of August 21st.  Catastrophic flooding occurred in all of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water from flash flooding across the county, along with the flooding from the Blanchard River in Findlay, persisted for several days across the area. See the accompanying write ups for more details about the flooding in the flash flood write up for Hancock County, including damage estimates. There is a separate write up for details of the Blanchard River gage at Findlay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin interacted with a stationary front to cause heavy rain producing thunderstorms over portions of northern Ohio.  The thunderstorms trained across Wyanodot, Hancock, Crawford and Richland Counties during the early morning hours of August 21st.  Catastrophic flooding occurred in all of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across the area during the early morning hours of the 21st quickly brought the Blanchard River in Findlay into flood. At 1:00 am on the 21st, the river gage reading was only 1.82 feet. As the heavy rain fell across the area, the river climbed at the rate of nearly two feet per hour, and exceeded the 11.0 foot flood stage during the early morning hours. As the day progressed, the river continued to climb and by the evening of the 21st its reading was around 15.0 feet. With much of the runoff basin impacted by the heavy rain, the river continued to climb even further during the overnight hours into the morning of the 22nd. By the morning of the 22nd, the river was approaching historical crest levels. Unfortunately, more heavy rain fell across the area during the morning hours of the 22nd, which only prolonged the process of draining the water from the area. The Blanchard River at Findlay finally crested at 18.46 feet at 1:00 pm on the 22nd. According to records this is the second highest crest in history at Findlay, with 18.50 feet on March 13th, 1913 holding onto the highest crest. With the rain coming to an end, the river was able to gradually fall over the next several days. Finally, during the midday hours of the 24th, the river fell below flood stage. See the flash flood write up for Hancock County for damage estimates for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving squall line caused damage in many parts of northern Indiana before clipping portions of Van Wert county. There were reports of damaging winds and intense lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a 40 foot oak tree, causing it to drop onto a home at the intersection of Payne and Convoy roads. Significant damage resulted to the home, but no injuries were reported. Damage is estimated at $65,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving squall line caused damage in many parts of northern Indiana before clipping portions of Van Wert county. There were reports of damaging winds and intense lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of horsebarn was lifted and carried 400 feet as winds entered through a large open door on west side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the morning hours of the 20th.  Flash flooding quickly developed causing roads and highways to become flooded and closed. As many as thirty roads were closed throughout southern portions of the county. The hardest hit areas were Spencer and Westfield Townships. Granger Township, north of Wadsworth, was also hit hard with flash flooding with roads closed there. Rainfall reports from that morning include 3.87 inches of rain in Seville between 9 am and 10 am. In the City of Medina, between 4 am and 4 pm, 2.79 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the morning of the 20th causing flash flooding conditions. Areas most impacted were from Norton east to Barberton, and from Fairlawn south to Clinton. Reports indicated there were many roads closed throughout western portions of Summit County. In Fairlawn, trained spotters reported flood waters affecting several homes. In Norton, an elementary school was threatened by flash flooding. Teachers sand bagged doors to prevent water from getting into the school. Rainfall reports from that day indicate that between 1.5 and 4.0 inches of rain fell across the area. However, a few reports from the Portage Lakes area indicate that as much as 4.0 inches of rain fell across the area over the 24 hour period ending around 4pm on the 20th. Further investigation of rainfall reports shows that one reporting station in Portage Lakes measured 1.5 inches of rain between 9:30 am and 10:00 am that morning alone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain fell across the area beginning during the morning hours of the 20th.  At 8:00 am on the 20th, the river stage at Woodville was 2.87 feet. The river quickly reacted to the heavy rain across the area and rose between 1 and 3 feet per hour during the afternoon of the 20th.  The river exceeded flood stage of 9.0 feet during the evening hours of the 20th.  The river remained in flood through the 21st, and crested at around 11.6 feet at around 11:00 pm on the 21st.  Despite the rain tapering off in northwest portions of Sandusky County, another round of heavy rain moved through southern Wood and Hancock Counties during the overnight hours and into the morning of the 21st. This second round of heavy rain impacted the upstream runoff basins that drain into the Portage River. Additionally, another short period of heavy rain fell during the morning hours of the 22nd. The significant amount of runoff draining into the Portage River well upstream kept the river in flood for several days. It did not fall below flood stage until nearly three and a half days later, during the early morning hours of the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front interacted with a very unstable and sheared atmosphere allowed the development of several rotating storms. Some wind damage occurred but none associated with any tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the morning hours of the 20th.  Water was able to quickly drain into area streams and rivers, but by the midday hours, water began overwhelming creeks and streams. By the early afternoon hours, water was reported across roads in Pemberville. As the afternoon and evening progressed, reports were received of roads closed throughout the county. High water continued through the overnight across the county as reports from the morning of the 21st indicated that many roads were still flooded and closed. Water receded across the area through much of the day on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Erin interacted with a stationary front to cause heavy rain producing thunderstorms over portions of northern Ohio.  The thunderstorms trained across Wyanodot, Hancock, Crawford and Richland Counties during the early morning hours of August 21st.  Catastrophic flooding occurred in all of these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms affected Wyandot County during the late evening hours of August 20th and early morning hours of August 21st.  Rainfall rates with the strongest storms exceeded three inches per hour.  Up to six inches of rain was reported in the Upper Sandusky area by 4 am.  A Cooperative Observer in Upper Sandusky reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 9.35 inches at 7 am with most of this rain falling between 11 pm and 4 am.  An automated gauge at Kildeer Plain State Park recorded 4.10 inches. Runoff from this rain combined with ground already saturated from earlier rains led to catastrophic flooding across portions of Wyandot County.  The Carey area was especially hard hit as flooding destroyed or heavily damaged several dozen homes.  A total of 176 homes and 42 businesses in the town were damaged.  9 of 15 homes along West South Street were destroyed.  By 3 am the flood waters were over knee deep in many locations and many streets were closed.  Significant flooding was also reported in the Upper Sandusky and Sycamore areas.  Near Sycamore, two teenagers had to be rescued after they were swept into Sycamore Creek by surging flood waters.  Hundreds of people had to be evacuated from their homes in the county, including several dozen from a nursing home in Carey.  A total of 159 homes are believed to have been destroyed or sustained more than 50 percent losses in the county.  Over 500 additional homes and businesses sustained lesser damages from the flooding.  At the peak of the flooding, around daybreak on the 21st, nearly every federal, state and county road in the county was closed because of flooding.  Damage to roads, bridges and culverts was extensive.  Cleanup and overtime costs incurred by local government agencies was substantial.  In the City of Carey alone, nearly 600 tons of flood debris had to be removed.  Finally, standing water and erosion from the runoff caused damage to agricultural interests in the county.   |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall occurred across parts of northwestern Ohio, beginning early on the 20th and continuing through the 22nd. Much of this rainfall fell into the Maumee River basin, with the Blanchard River near Ottawa suffering the worst affects of the rainfall, which totaled upwards of 15 inches in some areas. Flooding started out rather general across much of the county with numerous road closures and some evacuations. However, the greatest damage occurred in and around the town of Ottawa which was completely underwater as a result of a near record crest of the Blanchard River at 31.7 feet. The most recent crest which caused significant flooding of the town was June 15, 1981 when the river crested at 29.75 feet. The record crest for this site is 33.3 feet set on March 13, 1913.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that numerous roads were closed due to flowing water from the heavy rains. In addition, the Blanchard River was rapidly rising and beginning to close off roads around the river. Damage estimates are for the flash flooding only and do not account for river flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary oriented from west to east stretched from the Plains into central Ohio. Rain with embedded thunderstorms moved across central Ohio during the early overnight hours of the 16th. A storm that moved through Ashland County became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a roof on a grocery store was blown off. The grocery store was located on the north side of Ashland. Trees were also reported down in the area. A survey team followed up on the report and found a significant portion of the grocery store's roof was blown off. Also noted was a barn southwest of the store that had a portion of its roof blown off. Numerous trees were down mainly between State Routes 511 and 58. Two semi trailers and a construction trailer were blown onto their sides near State Route 511 and U.S. 250.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was oriented west to east across central Ohio. The surface frontal boundary, in combination with upper level atmospheric support, allowed for severe thunderstorms to develop across the region. Many of the thunderstorms that day were severe, and even a few tornadoes developed across northern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey was conducted and an EF1 tornado was confirmed in northeastern Ashland County. The tornado touched down northeast of Sullivan along Black River School Road just to the west of the Medina County line.  Two school buildings were damaged by the tornado. One of the schools lost a section of roofing over a gymnasium. Sections of roofing along the remainder of the building were lifted and buckled by tornado winds. Significant amounts of rain water got into the building as a result of this roof damage. Water damage in the school was extensive and the gymnasium floor will need replacement. A modular building used as a classroom also sustained damage. Fences surrounding recreational fields at the school were also damaged. A house across the road from the schools was slightly damaged and a communication tower nearby was toppled. Many trees were downed. The tornado had a damage path around one half mile in length and about 30 yards in width.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved through northeast Ohio during the morning hours of the 23rd. Portions of this line of storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and large limb were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved through the Great Lakes region during the afternoon and evening hours of the 24th. Ahead of this cold front thunderstorms developed, some of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As severe thunderstorms moved through the area, trees, large limbs and power lines were reported down from West Toledo to Point Place. Trained spotters estimated wind gusts to be between 55 and 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall occurred across parts of northwestern Ohio, beginning early on the 20th and continuing through the 22nd. Much of this rainfall fell into the Maumee River basin, with the Blanchard River near Ottawa suffering the worst affects of the rainfall, which totaled upwards of 15 inches in some areas. Flooding started out rather general across much of the county with numerous road closures and some evacuations. However, the greatest damage occurred in and around the town of Ottawa which was completely underwater as a result of a near record crest of the Blanchard River at 31.7 feet. The most recent crest which caused significant flooding of the town was June 15, 1981 when the river crested at 29.75 feet. The record crest for this site is 33.3 feet set on March 13, 1913.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads closed across the county caused by high, flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the morning hours of the 20th.  Flash flooding conditions quickly developed during the mid morning hours as many roads between Hartville and Alliance were closed. A spotter in Hartville reported 3.05 inches of rain between 9:30 am and 11:00 am, with 2.0 inches falling just between 9:30 am and 10:30 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the morning hours of the 20th.  Flash flooding conditions quickly developed during the mid morning hours with basements flooding, and roads becoming flooding causing cars to become stranded. A commercial building was flooded because the water got so high in the street that passing vehicles created waves. These waves hit up against the front of the building and eventually water got inside the building. During the afternoon a vehicle rescue was performed on someone trapped in floodwaters. By the afternoon, a rainfall report from Beloit measured 2.81 inches of rain since 4:00 am. Since the afternoon of the 19th, a total of 3.09 inches of rain fell there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the morning hours of the 20th.  Rainfall reports indicate that between 2.0 and 4.0 inches of rain fell during the morning hours, and rainfall rates during this time approached 2.0 inches per hour in Gibsonburg. By late morning, an underpass was closed in Bellevue. The rain tapered off during the afternoon hours of the 20th, but runoff from the heavy rain continued. The heavy runoff quickly swamped streams and rivers across the area. As a result, water gradually backed up causing flooding conditions across the county. Many roads became flooded, and remained that way through the overnight hours into the 21st. The water slowly receded through the day on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the early morning hours of the 20th.  Flash flooding conditions were reported during the morning hours as creeks quickly overflowed their banks and underpasses filled with three feet of water making those roads impassable. By the midday hours, flooding was reported in Bettsville and Old Fort with many roads closed including State Route 17 in Bettsville, and State Route 18 between Republic and Bellevue. During the afternoon hours, widespread flooding was reported with roads closed countywide, and evacuations were taking place in Bettsville. Flooding conditions continued through the overnight and morning hours of the 21st, but water began to recede across the area during the afternoon hours. However, runoff eventually brought the Sandusky River in Tiffin into flood. See the accompanying write up for additional information on the flooding of the Sandusky River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the morning hours of the 20th. Rainfall reports indicate that between 2.0 and 3.0 inches of rain fell that morning across the area. The heavy rain was able to quickly make its way to local streams and rivers, but those eventually filled up through the morning hours, and excess runoff began ponding in low lying locations and roads throughout the county. By midday of the 20th, roads northeast of Bellevue to Huron gradually flooded. Flooding continued through the overnight hours into the 21st.  By the morning, water eventually flooded out the Franklin Flats area. Floodwaters receded throughout the county during the midday and afternoon hours of the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare October heat wave, during the 1st and 2nd weeks of the month, helped peak the severity of the drought. ||On the 11th, Gallia County declared an emergency due to a water shortage.  With the lowering of the water table, wells were becoming less productive. ||Morgan County officials reported that their wildlife was being stressed from the lack of available water.  Deer were dying from the effects of the drought and a dry weather disease.||Much needed and widespread rain finally arrived on the 23rd and the 24th.  Rain amounts of 2 to 3 inches were common.  As the growing season ended and the autumn foliage peaked, drought conditions began to abate or ease.

A cold front was located from the central Great Lakes south through the Mississppi River Valley during the evening hours of the 25th. A warm, moist airmass over the region allowed for a few severe thunderstorms to developed during the evening and early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds created a damage path about one hundred yards wide and about four miles long. Along this path, numerous trees and large limbs were reported down. Several buildings had roof damage, and a box trailer was blown over by the severe winds.

A cold front moved through the area during the afternoon and evening hours of the 8th. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front, and some of the thunderstorms became severe. Flooding conditions also occurred as the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large limbs, several trees and power lines were reported down around Sandusky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was located from the central Great Lakes south through the Mississppi River Valley during the evening hours of the 25th. A warm, moist airmass over the region allowed for a few severe thunderstorms to developed during the evening and early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down from Auburn Center to Newbury. Law enforcement also reported that additional trees were down throughout the entire county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was located from the central Great Lakes south through the Mississppi River Valley during the evening hours of the 25th. A warm, moist airmass over the region allowed for a few severe thunderstorms to developed during the evening and early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio during the late evening hours of the 26th, and continued into the 27th. A cold front was located well west of the area, but thunderstorms were able to develop with the help of a disturbance aloft moving through the area. The cold front moved through the area late on the 27th and early on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three day rain totals, along a front, reached into the 2 to 2.25 inch range across Perry and Morgan Counties.  Small streams overflowed across northern portions of Perry County.  The small streams in the Jonathan and Painter Creek basins flooded and closed roads.  In Morgan County, the creeks feeding into the swollen Muskingum River flooded.  Again, roads were flooded and closed.  No evacuations were required in either county.

An area of strong low pressure moved northeast across northern Ohio during the evening hours of January 5th, 2007.  Moderate to heavy rainfall accompanied this storm system.  Most of northern Ohio saw between one and two inches of rainfall on the 5th and 6th with localized amounts up to three inches reported.  Runoff from this heavy rainfall caused streams and rivers in northern Ohio to rapidly rise.  Moderate to major flooding was reported at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains on January 5th caused the Blanchard River in Findlay to go above the flood stage of 11 feet during the early morning hours of January 6th.  The river crested at 14.78 feet during the evening hours of the 7th and fell back below flood stage just before daybreak on the 8th.  Significant flooding occurred along the river in Findlay.  Several businesses in the downtown area were damaged by flooding.  Many streets and roads had to be closed.  Streets along Eagle Creek and Lye Creek were also hard hit by flooding.  Dozens of homes sustained significant damage from flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in advance of a cold front.  A few storms pulsed to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Route 757.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into northern Ohio causing widespread showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours.  Many of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was located from the central Great Lakes south through the Mississppi River Valley during the evening hours of the 25th. A warm, moist airmass over the region allowed for a few severe thunderstorms to developed during the evening and early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region causing scattered thunderstorms to develop.  A few of the stronger storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds downed a couple of large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 8th. Numerous severe thunderstorms developed ahead and along this cold front. Severe weather occurred throughout most counties in the area stretching from Lucas County to Mahoning County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced severe weather across portions of Western Pennsylvania, Southeast Ohio, and Northern West Virginia. As low pressure slowly moved east of the region, heavy rains with some snow melt then caused flooding of some streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in Caldwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across northern Ohio causing heavy rain producing thunderstorms to develop.  Flash flooding and severe weather occurred in Northwest Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms moved across central and south central Ohio during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front caused showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region. Many storms became severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the West interacted with a warm and humid air mass provoking the development of showers and thunderstorms across the region. Several storms became severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EMA reported trees down West of Cambridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into northern Ohio causing widespread showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours.  Many of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stretched from west to east across northern Ohio during the early morning hours of the 7th. This frontal boundary was associated with low pressure centered over the Plains. Through the day on the 7th, this boundary moved north allowing a warm and humid airmass to set up over the region. Thunderstorms developed across the area during the morning hours of the 7th, and again during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper-level low over the Northeast caused showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region. Many storms became severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down along Rt. 518.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area during the afternoon and evening hours of the 8th. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front, and some of the thunderstorms became severe. Flooding conditions also occurred as the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads across northern portions of the county were flooded, especially in Greene, Gustavus, and Kinsman Townships. Some of the flooded roads included State Routes 87 and 193. In Greene Township, basement flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into northern Ohio causing widespread showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours.  Many of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms affected southwest and central Ohio ahead of a strong cold front during the late evening of the 18th and the early morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from penny to quarter sized occurred from downtown Cincinnati north and east into the Norwood area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Ohio during the afternoon hours of the 13th. The severe thunderstorms developed as an upper level disturbance moved over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours of the 19th. Ahead of this cold front in a very warm and moist airmass, severe thunderstorms developed during the midday hours. The activity continued through the afternoon before diminishing during the evening hours as the cold front moved east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across the area during the morning of the 20th and caused flash flooding across primarily northeast portions of the county. Some homes had water flowing into the basements, and a strip mall in the area was also flooded. Enough homes in the area were flooded out that a shelter was opened in Rittman for the flood victims. Rainfall amounts varied significantly across the northern half of Wayne County for this event. While the Wayne County Airport picked up only 0.86 inches between 4 pm Sunday and 4 pm Monday, rainfall reports just north of Rittman into southern Medina County indicate that between 2.0 and 4.0 inches of rain fell there during that same time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary stretched from west to east through northern Ohio during the morning hours of the 20th, and remained nearly stationary for the next several days. Heavy rain along this boundary moved across far northern Ohio during the day causing flooding and flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell across the area during the morning hours of the 20th quickly filled streams and rivers across the area. At 9 am on the 20th, the Huron River at Milan was 7.74 feet. As the morning progressed, the river rose between two and three feet per hour until it rose above the flood stage of 18 feet during the afternoon. The river continued to rise through the evening hours, and crested at 22.7 feet at around 10:45 pm. Through the overnight hours into the 21st, the river gradually fell, but did not drop below flood stage until the late morning hours of the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was oriented west to east across central Ohio. The surface frontal boundary, in combination with upper level atmospheric support, allowed for severe thunderstorms to develop across the region. Many of the thunderstorms that day were severe, and even a few tornadoes developed across northern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening as an upper level disturbance moved through the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were downed.
